content:
Go makes concurrency look free. You have a list of things to do, you write go doThing(x) in a loop, and suddenly everything runs at once. A goroutine costs a couple of kilobytes; you can have a million of them. So the first time you need to do a lot of network calls in parallel, the obvious code is to just launch one goroutine per call and let the runtime sort it out.
Then you point it at something real — say, every port on a few hundred hosts — and it dies. Not slowly: it falls over with dial tcp: socket: too many open files, or your machine grinds to a halt, or the far end starts refusing you. The goroutines were never the problem. What’s scarce is everything behind each goroutine: open sockets, file descriptors, connection-table slots. A million goroutines all trying to hold a live TCP connection at the same instant is a million file descriptors, and your OS gave you 1,024 by default.
This is the lesson behind “bounded concurrency,” and it’s worth learning on a concrete build. We’ll write a small TCP port scanner — read targets from a file, check a set of ports, report what’s open…
